findings.message: `Bash(curl*)` is declared in allowed-tools but the command body contains only `task *` invocations — no direct curl call is shown
Vague quantifier: "at least some servers are 'ready'" — does not specify the minimum count or which servers are required
Vague qualifier: "actionable troubleshooting steps" — does not specify what makes a step actionable
Command uses $ARGUMENTS for issue type but has no empty-input branch — when invoked with no argument, "Issue Type: " renders blank with no guidance on how to proceed
Vague quantifier: "Saves significant tokens per `initialize`" — "significant" is unquantified
Vague qualifier: "Explain the chosen route briefly" — "briefly" is subjective and does not constrain output length or format
Four workflow rules listed as unordered bullets instead of numbered steps — multi-step commands should use numbered steps for unambiguous ordering
No output format section — command does not specify what form the final response should take (report, checklist, code, etc.)
Vague directive: "prefer Playwright CLI guidance" — "prefer" does not specify when the preference applies or how strong it is
Usage comment documents `curl -fsSL .../install.sh | bash` as the standard installation method with no integrity check — users who follow the docs execute arbitrary code from GitHub

Downloads remote `airis-gateway` shell script from GitHub raw URL and immediately executes it (chmod +x at line 119, executed at line 129) without any hash verification — functionally equivalent to curl | bash
`curl -fsSL "$url" | tar -xz -C "$BIN_DIR"` downloads and unpacks the airis-workspace binary from GitHub releases without checksum or signature verification
`VERSION="${AIRIS_VERSION:-latest}"` defaults to `latest` — any future push to the main/latest tag silently changes what new installs receive, with no pinned version in the distributed script

Downloads `airis_bootstrap.py` from GitHub raw URL without integrity check; the file is executed at line 358 via `python3 "$DIR/scripts/airis_bootstrap.py"` — remote code execution if the GitHub repo or CDN is compromised

`server = '$server'` interpolates the shell variable directly into a python3 -c heredoc; a server name containing a single quote or command substitution can corrupt or hijack the embedded Python code in cmd_enable (same pattern at line 310 in cmd_disable)
`BASE_URL` is constructed with `${AIRIS_VERSION:-latest}` — all remote file downloads (docker-compose, configs, scripts) resolve against the latest tag without a pinned version
Usage comment documents `curl -fsSL .../quick-install.sh | bash` as the standard installation method with no integrity check — same supply-chain risk as install.sh
`curl -fsSL "$url" | tar -xz -C "$install_dir"` downloads airis-workspace binary without checksum verification — same pattern as install.sh:163
No example blocks — skill provides no concrete before/after examples of the workflow in action
Vague quantifier: "appropriate caution" — does not specify what caution level is appropriate for a given operation
No example blocks — skill has no concrete debugging scenario walkthrough

Skill directs users to invoke `superpowers:systematic-debugging` before using this skill, but `superpowers` is not declared as a dependency in `skills/.claude-plugin/plugin.json` — users without superpowers installed receive broken workflow guidance
No example blocks — skill has no concrete implementation walkthrough
No example blocks — skill has no concrete research scenario walkthrough
Usage comment documents `curl -fsSL .../install.sh | bash` as the standard installation method with no integrity check — users who follow the docs execute arbitrary code from GitHub
Usage comment documents `curl -fsSL .../quick-install.sh | bash` as the standard installation method with no integrity check — same supply-chain risk as install.sh

Downloads remote `airis-gateway` shell script from GitHub raw URL and immediately executes it (chmod +x at line 119, executed at line 129) without any hash verification — functionally equivalent to curl | bash

`curl -fsSL "$url" | tar -xz -C "$BIN_DIR"` downloads and unpacks the airis-workspace binary from GitHub releases without checksum or signature verification
`curl -fsSL "$url" | tar -xz -C "$install_dir"` downloads airis-workspace binary without checksum verification — same pattern as install.sh:163

`server = '$server'` interpolates the shell variable directly into a python3 -c heredoc; a server name containing a single quote or command substitution can corrupt or hijack the embedded Python code in cmd_enable (same pattern at line 310 in cmd_disable)
`VERSION="${AIRIS_VERSION:-latest}"` defaults to `latest` — any future push to the main/latest tag silently changes what new installs receive, with no pinned version in the distributed script
`BASE_URL` is constructed with `${AIRIS_VERSION:-latest}` — all remote file downloads (docker-compose, configs, scripts) resolve against the latest tag without a pinned version
Four workflow rules listed as unordered bullets instead of numbered steps — multi-step commands should use numbered steps for unambiguous ordering
No output format section — command does not specify what form the final response should take (report, checklist, code, etc.)
Vague directive: "prefer Playwright CLI guidance" — "prefer" does not specify when the preference applies or how strong it is
No example blocks — skill provides no concrete before/after examples of the workflow in action
Vague quantifier: "appropriate caution" — does not specify what caution level is appropriate for a given operation
No example blocks — skill has no concrete debugging scenario walkthrough
No example blocks — skill has no concrete implementation walkthrough
No example blocks — skill has no concrete research scenario walkthrough
Command uses $ARGUMENTS for issue type but has no empty-input branch — when invoked with no argument, "Issue Type: " renders blank with no guidance on how to proceed
Vague quantifier: "at least some servers are 'ready'" — does not specify the minimum count or which servers are required
Vague qualifier: "actionable troubleshooting steps" — does not specify what makes a step actionable
`Bash(curl*)` is declared in allowed-tools but the command body contains only `task *` invocations — no direct curl call is shown
Vague qualifier: "Explain the chosen route briefly" — "briefly" is subjective and does not constrain output length or format
Vague quantifier: "Saves significant tokens per `initialize`" — "significant" is unquantified

Skill directs users to invoke `superpowers:systematic-debugging` before using this skill, but `superpowers` is not declared as a dependency in `skills/.claude-plugin/plugin.json` — users without superpowers installed receive broken workflow guidance
